alt: Photo by MedGenome on April 12, 2026. May be an image of text that says '串 MEDGENOME ClonoTrack: NGS- NGS-Based -Based Clonality & MRD Monitoring in B-Cell Malignancies'.
caption:
Read the full blog, link in bio! 

Minimal Residual Disease (MRD) is no longer just a research metric, it is a critical determinant of prognosis and treatment strategy in B-cell malignancies. With advancements in Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS), detecting residual disease at ultra-low levels (up to10⁻⁶ ) is now possible, enabling earlier intervention and more informed clinical decisions.

 MedGenome’s ClonoTrack brings this capability to clinical practice through:

1. High-resolution clonality assessment
2. Longitudinal MRD monitoring
3. Precision-driven care across B-ALL, CLL, MM, and NHL

 Explore how NGS-based MRD tracking is reshaping disease management and improving patient outcomes.

#MedGenome #ClonoTrack #Oncology #NGS #Hematology #BcellMalignancies #MRD #PrecisionMedicine

caption:
Featured on BW Healthcare World for World Hemophilia Day: Link in bio

Dr. Thenral S Geetha, Principal Scientist - Inherited Disease Genomics, MedGenome, discusses the impact of delayed hemophilia diagnosis in children.

Early signs like unexplained bruising or prolonged bleeding are often overlooked, and limited awareness and access to specialised testing further contribute to this gap. Advances in genetic testing are now enabling earlier detection and more timely care.

With increased awareness and the right diagnostic support, earlier detection can significantly improve disease management and quality of life for affected children.

caption:
Featured on IndiaMedToday for International Women’s Day

To read the full article, click the link in bio.

Dr. Priya Kadam, Director, Reproductive Genomics, MedGenome, discusses how fertility gradually changes with age and how understanding genetic factors can help women make more informed decisions about pregnancy after 30.

Over time, the quantity of eggs and their chromosomal quality naturally decline, which can influence conception and pregnancy outcomes. Advances in genetic screening, such as carrier screening, embryo testing during IVF, and early prenatal screening, now allow potential risks to be identified earlier.

With the right information and guidance, women can approach fertility planning with greater clarity, confidence, and awareness.

#InternationalWomensDay #WomensHealth #FertilityAwareness #Genomics #Pregnancy

caption:
Featured on India TV for World Parkinson's Day. (Link in bio) 

Dr. Ramesh Menon, Director of Personal Genomics and Genomic Medicine at MedGenome, explains how genetics shapes Parkinson’s Disease (PD) risk. While family history plays a role, cases can also often result from a combination of genetic and environmental factors.

In India, an estimated 32–45% of PD cases are early-onset (<50 years), underscoring the need for timely insights and clinical guidance. Advances in genomics are now enabling earlier risk assessment—helping individuals make more informed decisions about monitoring and long-term neurological health.

When something is rare, the journey can feel isolating.
For many families, the hardest part is not just the symptoms, but the uncertainty. The unanswered questions. The years of searching.
This Rare Disease Day, MedGenome stands with every patient and every family navigating that path. MedGenome’s advanced genomic diagnostics can help uncover the underlying genetic cause, bringing clarity, direction, and a starting point for care.

Because rare does not mean alone. And it should never mean invisible.

caption:
Featured in Fortune India: Click link in bio

This World Health Day 2026, we reaffirmed our commitment to science-led innovation in healthcare with India’s first prospective study demonstrating the clinical impact of our cost-effective RNA-based NGS in Sarcoma diagnosis.

The study highlights how integrating RNA sequencing into clinical workflows can significantly improve diagnostic accuracy and influence treatment decisions in complex sarcoma cases, marking an important step towards strengthening precision oncology in India, especially in resource-constrained settings.

You can read the study in the Journal of Global Oncology: https://ascopubs.org/doi/10.1200/GO-25-00440 (link in bio)

As we move forward, science and collaboration will remain central to improving patient outcomes in rare and challenging cancers.

#WorldHealthDay #PrecisionMedicine #Oncology #Genomics #CancerCare #Sarcoma #NGS #HealthcareInnovation #MedGenome #ScienceForHealth

#AnswersThatMatter

Genomics is transforming cancer care today, in two crucial ways – early diagnosis and targeted treatments. Dr. Vedam Ramprasad, CEO of MedGenome, highlights that genomics enables early detection of genetic mutations linked to cancer. This allows for timely medical interventions like regular monitoring and managing lifestyle habits that could trigger the disease. It also helps clinicians identify the precise gene mutation and tailor treatment plans targeted toward the exact mutation.

Learn how genomics is enhancing clinical processes and improving patient outcomes. Watch the full video, link in bio.

A family history of cancer translates to a significantly increased risk of the disease for a person, making early risk assessment an important part of preventive care. 

Speaking to @India Today TV, Vedam Ramprasad, PhD, CEO of MedGenome, explains that certain gene mutations linked to cancer can be inherited across generations. Genomic testing helps identify these markers, enabling a better understanding of the risk and informed measures toward surveillance and early intervention for individuals and families.

Discover how mutations in genes affect generations in a family. Watch the full video, link in bio.

caption:
Featured on Times Now for Glaucoma Awareness Month

Read the full article here: https://www.timesnownews.com/health/glaucomas-silent-vision-loss-how-genetics-is-transforming-early-detection-article-153537902 

Dr. Soumittra N, Disease Head - Ophthalmology, MedGenome, highlights how vision loss due to Glaucoma begins long before a person notices any change, making it one of the leading causes of irreversible blindness worldwide. Early diagnosis can help slow or halt disease progression, preserving vision and quality of life.

Glaucoma is often referred to as the silent thief of sight, as it progresses quietly, without pain or early warning signs. In many cases, genetics plays a significant role in determining who is at risk. These challenges call for a shift from late-stage treatment to early risk detection. 

Because when it comes to glaucoma, awareness and timely action are the strongest tools we have to protect our eyesight.

#glaucomaawarenessmonth #eyehealthawareness #preventblindness #earlydetectionmatters #visioncare #geneticrisk #ophthalmology #healthyeyes

In bloodstream infections, clinical outcomes depend on precise identification—requiring broad, high-sensitivity pathogen detection.

Introducing MetaSeq, powered by DISQVER®. A next-generation, cell-free, culture-free metagenomic NGS test for unbiased pathogen detection from a single blood sample.
Built on Noscendo’s DISQVER® metagenomics and reference database, MetaSeq uses a proprietary algorithm to deliver clinically actionable pathogen detection from cell-free DNA.

With coverage of 1,400+ pathogens, MetaSeq enables faster, more confident clinical decisions in complex and critical cases.

Detecting Minimal Residual Disease (MRD) is critical for improving outcomes and enabling effective patient management in NPM1-mutated AML. MedGenome’s NPM1-MRD Detect, a precision qRT-PCR–based assay, delivers ELN-aligned reports with highly sensitive quantification (<2 copies) and real-time disease monitoring, backed by NGS-confirmed validation. This World Diabetes Day, Dr Subramanian Kannan, Consultant Endocrinologist at Narayana Health Bangalore, shares an important message. Many people in India are now getting diabetes at a young age and the reasons can differ from person to person.

Some may have type 1 diabetes, some may have early type 2 diabetes, which could be a multi-gene or polygenic condition, or it could be monogenic or single gene related. In certain people, genetic testing can help doctors confirm monogenic diabetes. This is useful because some of these patients respond very well to targeted medicines.

Genetic testing can also help doctors plan treatment during pregnancy and understand the risk for children or other family members.

Diabetes is not the same for everyone. Knowing the correct type can make treatment safer and more effective.

Dr. Ramesh Menon, Director of Personal Genomics and Genomic Medicine, MedGenome, talks about how genetics shapes the risk, onset, and severity of diabetic complications.

While diabetes is commonly viewed through the lens of blood sugar control, Dr. Menon highlights that genetic variations can influence how the disease can lead to complications such as retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy, and cardiovascular diseases, even among individuals managing their diabetes well. Genetic testing can empower us to comprehensively understand our condition and effectively prevent and manage diabetes and its complications.

If you have a family history of any health conditions, talk to your clinician to learn more about genetic tests and ensure healthier futures for you and your loved ones. You can also reach out to our genetic counsellors at https://diagnostics.medgenome.com/medgenome-genetic-counseling/

Dr. Thangarajan Rajkumar, Director of Research (Oncology), MedGenome, sheds light on the growing importance of genetic awareness in understanding and managing prostate cancer, one of the most common cancers among men worldwide. He explains how specific genetic mutations, including BRCA1, BRCA2, DNA mismatch repair genes, and few other rare mutations can increase an individual’s susceptibility to the disease.

Dr. Rajkumar emphasizes that early screening, genetic testing, and timely intervention can make all the difference, especially for men with a family history of prostate, breast, or ovarian cancer. He further discusses how advancements like next-generation sequencing (NGS) and treatments such as PARP inhibitors and immunotherapy are revolutionizing prostate cancer care.

By adopting a preventive and informed approach, he stresses, men can take charge of their health — ensuring early detection, better treatment outcomes, and improved quality of life as they age.

Dr. Sakthivel Murugan, Vice President - Lab Operations, MedGenome, joined the panel of industry experts at the ET Healthcare Leaders Summit 2025 to discuss Genomics, Diagnostics, and Precision Medicine.

The conversation spanned from the evolution of preventive and proactive healthcare to technology integration, accessibility, and affordability of genetic diagnostics in India today, including key trends in genomics that are shaping the future of healthcare.

Discussing the advancements and future outlook for genomics, Dr. Murugan highlighted that a critical area of growth would be extending genomic diagnostics beyond urban centers to reach underserved rural populations, bridging the current accessibility gap. Moreover, he emphasized that a crucial shift towards reducing dependence on Western guidelines and developing India-centric guidelines for genomic testing and interpretation is the need of the hour. He said that this would recognize the unique genetic diversity and disease prevalence within the Indian population, ensuring more relevant and effective clinical applications.

On the panel, Dr. Murugan was joined by Dr. Om Manchanda, Former MD, Dr. Lal Path Labs, Dr. Harsh Mahajan, Founder & Managing Director, Mahajan Imaging, and Dr. Arjun Dang, CEO and Partner, Dr Dangs Lab. The session was moderated by Mr. Vikas Dandekar, Editor-Pharma & Healthcare, The Economic Times.

We were delighted to host over 200 clinicians and researchers at “Precision Oncology in Practice: From Bench to Bedside”, a symposium organised by MedGenome in collaboration with Illumina, on 22 August at The Lalit, Mumbai.

At the event, we launched the CNS Tumor Methylation Classifier – India’s first genome-wide methylation-based precision diagnostic test for CNS tumors.

The evening featured insights on precise classification of CNS tumors using DNA methylation, clinical applications in lung and hepatobiliary cancers, the role of HRD biomarkers, retrospective findings from hereditary cancer samples at MedGenome, as well as a panel discussion on the Methylation Classifier and the future of genomics-driven precision oncology in India.

We are delighted to share that Dr. Ravi Gupta, Vice President - Bioinformatics, MedGenome, participated in the CII Global Genomics Summit 2025 as a part of the expert panel that discussed "Building Genomics India Infrastructure – Bioinformatics, AI, Big Data, Biobanks, and Data Governance".

Sharing his insights, Dr. Ravi emphasized how bioinformatics tools are making processes more efficient, accurate, and faster today, spotlighting MedGenome's proprietary AI/ML-enabled tools that are used for the analysis of various complex diseases. He also discussed the need for standardizing genomic data in India to help future research in the field.

On the panel, Dr Ravi was joined by esteemed speakers, including Dr. Suchita Dayanand, Country Head, Illumina India, Dr. Hitesh Goswami, Cofounder and CEO, 4 Basecare, Dr Manoj Gopalkrishnan, CEO, Algorithmic Biologics, Mr. Bipin Pradeep Kumar, Co-founder, IOM Bioworks, Dr. Krishnadev Oruganty, Director - Innovations & Data Science, Bencos Research Solutions, and Vamsi Veeramachaneni, Chief Scientific Officer, Strand Life Sciences.

The event brought together thought leaders, industry experts, and policymakers for insightful conversations on advancements in the sector and optimizing the transformative power of genomic science. We extend our gratitude to Namita Kapoor Bahl and the CII team for organizing such an impactful event and inviting MedGenome to be part of it.

At MedGenome, we believe genomics holds immense potential to not just revolutionize healthcare but also strengthen India's innovation ecosystem and drive economic growth. As we work toward making genomics accessible for all, we look forward to more such collaborative events that fuel dialogue, partnerships, and progress.
